summary: I was born in Kingston, Jamaica, and lived there until I was 15 years old. My parents, Daniel and Ann Derrick, still live there, though my mother has never been to the United States. Growing up in Kingston, I learned farming and experienced a warmer climate than here in Florida. I attended school there up to the 4th grade before coming to the States.

When I first came to the United States at age 15, I landed in New York City before moving south. It was my entry point into this country, and from there I eventually made my way to Florida. New York City was just the beginning of my journey in America.

After arriving in New York City, I moved south to Florida where I found work and settled down. I worked at a sawmill near Haywood Falls and later moved my family to Plant City in 1924. Florida's climate was cooler than Jamaica's, but I grew to like the experience and worked hard to farm my land here.
cues: worked at sawmill near Haywood Falls
moved family to Plant City in 1924

summary: I worked at a sawmill in a place called Haywood Falls, which is near McFarland, Georgia. This was one of my first jobs after moving south from New York City. The work paid about $1.10 a week, and it was a tough but important experience for me.
cues: early job after New York City

summary: Haywood Falls, where I worked at the sawmill, is near McFarland, Georgia. I spent some time there before moving on to Florida. My father taught me how to raise hogs back in Georgia, so I have a connection to that area through work and family knowledge.
cues: father taught me hog raising in Georgia

summary: In 1924, I moved my family to Plant City, Florida, where we have lived since. My children Bernie and Albert were born in Cowes, but Elizabeth's family is from Plant City. I rent a small house here and farm about three acres, growing strawberries, beans, and other crops to support my family.

I attended school in the West Indies, specifically in Kingston, Jamaica, up to the 4th grade before coming to the United States. Farming is common in the West Indies, and I brought that knowledge with me when I moved here. The climate there is warmer, but I adapted to the cooler weather in Florida.

My son Albert has been in the Civilian Conservation Corps camp at Olustee, Florida since 1936. He is very responsible and sends part of his pay home every month to help support the family. I myself was transferred to the camp at Olustee during my time in the army and stayed there for nearly two years.
cues: Albert in CCC camp at Olustee since 1936
Albert sends part of pay home
I was transferred to Olustee camp

summary: Olustee, Florida is where the Civilian Conservation Corps camp is located, where my son Albert has been since 1936. I was also transferred there during my army service and spent nearly two years at that camp. I liked my time there and even gained weight while stationed at Olustee.

I served in the United States Army for a time, initially stationed in Tarpon Springs, Florida, before being transferred to the camp at Olustee. My son George Jr. also served in the army before working in the Poinciana grove. My army experience lasted nearly two years, and I enjoyed the discipline and routine.

During my time in the United States Army, I was stationed in Tarpon Springs, Florida before being transferred to Olustee. It was part of my military service, which lasted nearly two years. Tarpon Springs was one of the places where I gained valuable experience before moving on to other duties.
cues: part of nearly two years service
